key_insight: Across lung CT tasks, the best model setup depends more on the task and segmentation choice than on any single foundation model, with classifier choice especially important for survival, histology, and age prediction.
clinical_relevance: For practical lung cancer phenotyping, Curia with tumor segmentation and a CatBoost head is the safest overall default, while tumor-segmentation choices matter most for volume and stage assessment.
method: Benchmarked 5 feature extractors: Curia, Curia-2, DINOv3, Radiomics2D, Radiomics3D
Compared 7 classification heads and 3 segmentation regimes across 5 tasks
Used LUNG1 for training and internal/external test cohorts to assess cross-cohort robustness
evidence_signal: Models were trained on LUNG1 (n=338) and tested on an internal set (n=84) plus external LUNG2 (n=211), with worst-case cross-cohort performance as the main metric.
watch_item: Results are cohort-dependent and some gains may reflect label-derivation effects, so the recommended default should still be validated task-by-task before deployment.
paper_title: Foundation Models vs. Radiomics for Lung Computed Tomography: A Benchmark of Feature Extractors, Classification Heads, and Segmentation Choices
